questions.prompt: On the Ferris wheel, the highlighted rider's HEIGHT (above the wheel's center) over time traces out what shape?
hints: The rider goes around a circle, but you're tracking only one coordinate of their position.
Height rises and falls, smoothly and repeatedly, between extremes.
questions.explanation: The vertical position of a point rotating uniformly around a circle is, by definition, a sine wave (in units of the radius). That's what sine literally IS.

choices: It's an arbitrary trig rule you memorize.
Because $\sin$ and $\cos$ are the two coordinates of a point on a unit circle, so they satisfy the Pythagorean theorem.
Because $\sin$ and $\cos$ are inverses of each other.
Because both functions are bounded by 1.
hints: What two things does the Ferris wheel rider have at any instant?
The rider's $(x, y)$ position sits on a circle of radius 1 — Pythagorean theorem applies.
questions.explanation: $\cos\theta$ is the rider's x-coordinate; $\sin\theta$ is their y-coordinate. The rider is on a circle of radius 1, so $x^2 + y^2 = 1^2 = 1$. Substituting gives the identity.

questions.prompt: On the Ferris wheel, the rider's HORIZONTAL position over time traces out:
choices: The same sine wave as before
hints: Switch the widget to 'x-pos (cos)' or 'both' and watch.
The horizontal coordinate starts at the maximum when the rider is at 'three o'clock' — that's where cosine begins.
questions.explanation: The horizontal coordinate of a rotating point is the cosine. It has the same wave shape as sine but starts at 1 instead of 0 — they're 90° apart in phase.

questions.prompt: If you double the Ferris wheel's rotational speed, what happens to the sine wave on the right?
choices: It gets compressed horizontally — twice as many cycles per second (higher frequency).
hints: Speed = how fast we sweep through angles.
questions.explanation: Faster rotation means the rider completes a full cycle in less time, so the wave fits more cycles per second — that's a higher frequency. Amplitude (height of the wave) is set by the wheel's radius, not its speed.

questions.promptTex: \text{At angle } \theta = \pi/2 \text{ (a quarter turn from start), what is } \sin\theta?
hints: After a quarter turn counterclockwise from the 3 o'clock position, where is the rider?
Top of the wheel — maximum height.
questions.explanation: At $\theta = \pi/2$ the rider is at the top of the unit circle, so y-coordinate = 1. Hence $\sin(\pi/2) = 1$.
